coredata.dc:title: Lead-based crystal luminophores: Tuning emissive crystals and application for recording high temperature history with ratiometric and colorimetric signals
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Yongying
Wu, Yuandong
Mei, Dajiang
Qu, Yi
coredata.dc:description:
The development of luminescent crystal sensors for monitoring temperature changes and recording thermal history is still an interesting challenge because their wide applications in several industrial fields and their good thermal stability in harsh environments. Herein, we report a luminescent crystal sensor based on Lead-based complex for recording the high temperature history (>230 °C). Seven kinds of Lead-based complexes are designed and synthesized under an optimizing strategy for obtaining the crystal luminophores with high emissive brightness. Among them, complexes 1 (
Pb ( 2 ,
2
′
- bipy )
I
2
), 5 (
Pb ( 1 , 10 - phen )
I
2
) and 6 (
Pb
1 , 10 - phen
2
I
2
) show bright luminescence with the maximum emission at 595 nm, 565 nm and 592 nm, all of which use I− as the halide ligand. Thermogravimetric analysis results indicate that 5 and 6 show high degraded temperature at 230 and 330 °C, respectively. Combing with their different emissive colors, the transition process from 6 to 5 can be used to construct a high temperature recorder with the ratiometric response. Further experiments evidence that complex 6 can stably transits into 5 even at 270 °C through the partial degradation of the organic ligand. Notably, the emissive colors vary from orange (CIE coordination: 0.515, 0.472) to yellow (CIE coordination: 0.394, 0.554) that allows easy visualization by naked eyes. We believe that this work provides a stabilization strategy of luminescent complexes and extends their applications in more harsh industrial conditions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Luminescent crystals sensors
Lead halide complexes
High temperature
Thermal history
Colorimetric visualization

Pb

, (5) and
Pb ( 1 , 10 - phen )
SCN

, (7)
Single crystal structures and XRD patterns

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the designing strategy of luminescent lead-complexes and temperature recorder.
UV–vis-NIR diffuse reflectance spectra of 1–4(A) and 5–7(B).

(A,B): Emission spectra (A) and histogram (B) of 1–4 with 3 as a control; (C,D) Emission spectra (C) and histogram (D) of 5–7 with 1 as a control. (E): Fluorescence micrographs of 1, 2, 4 (top) and 5–7 (bottom), 3 showed too weak fluorescence to be obtained (bar: 1 mm).
Illustration of the design of a fluorescent thermal recorder. (A) Emission spectra of 5 and 6; (B) Inset scheme of thermal recorder based on 6–5 transformation.

(A) Emission spectra of 6 with annealed at different temperature; (B) Ratiometric response
(
I
592
/
I
546
)
of 6 at different temperature (No. 1: room temperature; No. 2: 200 °C; No. 3: 220 °C; No. 4: 230 °C; No. 5: 250 °C; No. 6: 270 °C).

The time-related decomposition process of 6 under 230 °C (A), 250 °C (B) and 270 °C; (D) Ratiometric response
(
I
592
/
I
546
)
of the time-related decomposition process of 6 at different temperature.

(A) Emission spectra and (B) ratiometric response
(
I
592
/
I
546
)
of 6 with annealed at 235 °C in a muffle roaster.
Bulk luminescent crystal sensor for thermal recording at high temperature (270 °C) in a time scale of 0–35 min: (A) emission spectra; (B) CIE diagram.

head.ce:title: Lead-based crystal luminophores: Tuning emissive crystals and application for recording high temperature history with ratiometric and colorimetric signals

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University of Engineering Science, 333 Longteng Road, 201620 Shanghai, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Seven lead-complexes were synthesized and their structure-related luminescent properties and thermal stabilities were discussed in this work. Based on these studies, 6 was selected and optimized for recording the thermal history, which could transit into 5 with obvious emissive color change in the range of 230–270 °C.

The development of luminescent crystal sensors for monitoring temperature changes and recording thermal history is still an interesting challenge because their wide applications in several industrial fields and their good thermal stability in harsh environments. Herein, we report a luminescent crystal sensor based on Lead-based complex for recording the high temperature history (>230 °C). Seven kinds of Lead-based complexes are designed and synthesized under an optimizing strategy for obtaining the crystal luminophores with high emissive brightness. Among them, complexes 1 (
Pb ( 2 ,
2
′
- bipy )
I
2
), 5 (
Pb ( 1 , 10 - phen )
I
2
) and 6 (
Pb
1 , 10 - phen
2
I
2
) show bright luminescence with the maximum emission at 595 nm, 565 nm and 592 nm, all of which use I− as the halide ligand. Thermogravimetric analysis results indicate that 5 and 6 show high degraded temperature at 230 and 330 °C, respectively. Combing with their different emissive colors, the transition process from 6 to 5 can be used to construct a high temperature recorder with the ratiometric response. Further experiments evidence that complex 6 can stably transits into 5 even at 270 °C through the partial degradation of the organic ligand. Notably, the emissive colors vary from orange (CIE coordination: 0.515, 0.472) to yellow (CIE coordination: 0.394, 0.554) that allows easy visualization by naked eyes. We believe that this work provides a stabilization strategy of luminescent complexes and extends their applications in more harsh industrial conditions.

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent crystal sensors have been developed during the last decades, which show excellent sensing performance in the minor region detection in a wide range of fields with the advantages of the highly ordered structure and controllable fabrication [1–6]. As a critical parameter of temperature in many scientific and industrial fields, lots of luminescent sensors with colorimetric and emissive responses were designed to record minor temperature changes or stagnation temperature in thermal histories, which provided the convenient and feasible method for non-invasive operation with high spatial and thermal resolution [7–10]. However, there is still a big challenge in developing these sensors with simple fabricating method and enough stability in different environments that extend their practical applications in several areas of interest, especially, in the high and low temperature regions always occurred in industrial processes and mechanotronics [11–14]. For example, the synthesis of many upconversion luminescent materials need the controllable temperature at 300 °C for several hours [15].

Recently, lead-based materials have been got remarkable achievement in many fields, such as catalysis, magnetism, solar cells and so on [16–21]. Lead materials with organic ligand possess distinctive properties inherited from the interaction of inorganic and organic components. Lead-based materials exhibited the potentiality to be optoelectronic materials because of their excellent optical property, as we known, lots of reports focused on the Lead-based perovskite solar cell, which acted as the important role in the third generation of solar cells [22–25]. Consideration of the flexible design of organic molecules and efficient ligand–metal charge transfer, Lead-based complexes were potential candidates of luminescent molecules and sensors within suitable optimizing strategies through varying both of the organic and inorganic ligands [1]. Notably, the higher thermal stability of these complexes made them very useful to design the molecular sensors working under high temperature conditions. Although many works reported the design and synthesis of these Lead-complexes [26,27], however, the optimization of their luminescent properties was still at the early step. In our previous work, we constructed a pure organic fluorescent thermometer, which achieved ratiometric recognition in the range of −40 °C–50 °C [28]. Further research showed that the pure ones were unstable in the high temperature conditions over 200 °C. Herein, we wanted introducing a metal core to improve the thermal stability of the fluorescent thermometer. Thus, we designed a series of Lead-complexes in order to explore the structure-dependent luminescent properties of these Lead-complexes and try to construct a luminescent sensor for recording thermal history at high temperature. As shown in Scheme 1 , seven Lead-complexes (1–7) with different halide ions and two kinds of organic ligands was designed and synthesized for optimizing their photophysical properties and then constructed a thermometer for high temperature. Among them, four anions
Cl
-
,
SCN
−
,
Br
−
,
I
−
were selected to explore their effects towards the luminescence efficiency of these complexes. Then, two organic ligands, the 2,2-dipyridine
( 2 ,
2
′
- dipy )
and 1,10-phenanthroline
( 1 , 10 - phen )
, were used to discuss the effects of their different rigidity on the luminescent property of complexes. For all complexes, the XRD, single-crystal X-ray diffraction and UV–vis-NIR spectra were used to identify their structures and band gaps. The luminescent studies indicated 5 with one 1,10-phenanthroline and two iodide ligands showed the most bright luminescence than other complexes. All complexes showed good thermal stability over 230 °C, in which the complexes with one organic ligand showed the higher degradation degree. As a result, 6 with two 1,10-phenanthroline ligands and two iodide ligands would degrade into 5, its analogue with only one 1,10-phenanthroline ligand, over 230 °C. Because both 5 and 6 showed different emissive colors, the ratiometric mode was suitable for naked-eye recognition in the practical industrial processes. We optimized the emission intensities of both complexes through adjusting their excitation wavelength (from 310 nm to 450 nm) and then constructed a ratiometric fluorescent recording process from 6 to 5 based on the temperature-dependent irreversible transition. The degrading mechanism was evidenced by XRD analysis covering all annealed samples. As far as we known, the luminescent sensors working in such high temperature were seldom reported except some pure inorganic rare-earth compounds. We believe this work could provide a useful proposal for investigating the industrial applications of the huge available library of Lead-complexes in above fields.

All reagents used as received without further purification. DMSO (AR, Aladdin Industrial Corporation, 99.8%), DMF (AR, Aladdin Industrial Corporation, Pesticide Analysis Grade), 2,2-bipy(AR, Aladdin Industrial Corporation, 99%),
Pb
NO
3
2
(AR, Chinese Pharmaceutical Shanghai Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd.), NaSCN (AR, Aladdin Industrial Corporation, 99.99%), HBr (AR, Aladdin Industrial Corporation, 48 wt%), HCl (AR, 36–38%, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd),
PbCl
2
(AR, Aladdin Industrial Corporation.), 1,10-phen (AR, Aladdin Industrial Corporation) Crystal data collections were conducted at room temperature on a Bruker D8 Quest diffractometer equipped with Mo K
α
radiation. Powder data collections were conducted at room temperature on a Bruker D8 Quest diffractometer equipped with Mo K
α
radiation. Thermogravimetric analysis collections were performed on a SDTQ600 TG-DSC Thermogravimetric Analyzer. Data of Solid-state UV–vis-NIR diffuse reflectance spectroscopy collections were conducted at room temperature on a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrophotometer.

The synthesis of
Pb
SCN
2
and
PbBr
2
was shown in Supporting information. Both obtained products were used directly at next experiments without further purification.

2,2’-bipy (0.34 g, 2.17 mmol) was added into a solution of
PbI
2
(0.5 g, 1.08 mmol) in DMF (5 mL). Then, the mixed solution was sealed in a 50 mL PTFE-lined steel autoclave. The autoclave was heated directly to 120 °C and held at this temperature for 24 h. The solution was cooled down to room temperature and produced a small amount of powders and fine yellow particles. Then, the supernatant solution was transferred into an opening vial for volatilizing the solvent under a slow rate. After several days, a large number of yellow massive crystals were precipitated from the solution to yield about 80% based on Pb.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
Pb ( 2 ,
2
′
- bipy )
Br
2
, (2),
Pb ( 2 ,
2
′
- bipy )
Cl
2
, (3) and
Pb
1 , 10 - phen
2
I
2
, (6)
ce:section.ce:para: Complexes 2, 3, and 6 were synthesized by the same method according to complex 1 under 120 °C. Additionally, we used DMSO as the solvent instead of DMF in synthesis of complex 3. The yields of these complexes were listed below: 2, 70%; 3, 80%; 6, 70%. The details of synthesis were given in Supporting information.

2,2’-bipy (0.196 g, 1.25 mmol) was added into a solution of
Pb
SCN
2
(0.2 g, 0.62 mmol) in DMF (0.8 mL).The mixed solution was placed in an ultrasound equipment for fully dissolving and then sealed in a 10 mL vial. The vial was heated to 60 °C under stirring for 3 h. Then, the solution was cooled down to room temperature and produced a small amount of powders and fine yellow particles. Then, the reaction system was opened and stored in a ventilator for volatilizing the solvent under a slow rate. After several days, a large number of transparent colorless bulk crystal was precipitated from the solution to yield about 60% based on Pb.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
Pb ( 1 , 10 - phen )
I
2
, (5) and
Pb ( 1 , 10 - phen )
SCN
2
, (7)
ce:section.ce:para: Complexes 5 and 7 were synthesized by the same method according to complex 4 under different reaction temperature: (5, 60 °C; 7, room temperature). Additionally, we used DMSO as the solvent instead of DMF in synthesis of complex 3. The yields of both complexes were listed below: 5, 70%; 7, 75%. The details of synthesis were given in Supporting information.

Crystals of all seven kinds of complexes were selected and mounted on the top of glass fibers. A narrow-frame method was used with scan widths of 0.50° in
ω
and an exposing time of 10
s / frame
to collect the whole sphere of data. All crystals data were integrated by the software SAINT, and corrected for Lorentz, air absorption and polarization. Structures were solved by direct methods by SHELX-2014 embedded in the WinGX-2014 crystallographic software package. Besides, all of non-hydrogen atoms have been refined by anisotropic displacement parameters and the other hydrogen atoms were treated using a riding mode. The final refined structures and space group were checked and calculated using the PLATON program.

The ground powder samples (400 mg for each sample) of complexes 1–7 were mounted on the sample holder and scanned in the
2 θ
range of 5–70° with a step size of 0.02° and a step time of 0.2 s.

The samples were ground with
BaSO
4
(standard for 100% reflectance) and prepared as the flat specimens. The instrumental resolutions were settled at 0.1 nm in the UV–vis range and 2 nm in the near-IR range. The measured range was settled from 200 nm to 800 nm. Absorption (
α / S
) data were calculated from the reflectance spectra using the Kubellka-Munk function,
α / S =
1 - R
2
/ 2 R
, where
α
is the absorption coefficient, and S is the scatting coefficient, R is the reflectance at a given wave number.
E
g
was calculated through the formula:
E
g
= h ν = hc / λ
. The band gap was determined as the intersection point between the energy axis at the absorption offset and the line extrapolated from the linear part of the absorption edge in a
α / S
2
versus E (eV) plot [29,30].

The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of complexes 1–7 were recorded on a L160-8000 spectrometer (PerkinElmer, MA) in the wavenumber range from 400 to 1400
cm
- 1
with a resolution of 4
cm
- 1
at room temperature. The samples were ground with dried KBr, then pressed into pellets for acquiring FTIR data collection.

Thermogravimetric analysis was performed on a SDTQ600 TG-DSC Thermogravimetric Analyzer. The samples were placed in alumina crucibles, which were heated at a rate of 10 °C/min in the range from room temperature to 800 °C (only the complex 4 was headed to 1000 °C) under flowing nitrogen gas. An empty alumina crucible was used as the reference during the measurements.

Luminescent spectra were measured on an Edinburgh FS-5 luminescence spectrometer with high stable Xe lamp (450 W). The absolutely fluorescent quantum yield were measured with a Photon Technology International instrument (PTI QM-40). A light integrating spheres serving as accessory device of the instrument was used in the measurements. Solid samples for luminescent measurements were placed on a plate. The luminescent imaging was performed by a FM-30 microscopy (Shanghai Hui Tong Optical Instrument Co. Ltd.) with a filter over 400 nm. For the high temperature recording experiments, the samples were heated under different temperature and then tested by a spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Single crystal structures and XRD patterns
ce:section.ce:para: Single crystal structures of 1–7 were got by the single crystal X-ray diffraction. The structure pictures of these complexes were presented in Fig. 1 and Fig. S1. The complexes had the similar crystal structures in the monoclinic space groups (C2/c, No.15 for 1, 2, 4, 5 and 7) and P21/c, No. 14 for 3). As shown in Fig. 1A and S1, these complexes formed the 1-D chain structures with the bipyridine rings or 1,10-phen rings distributed on both sides of the chains. The lead atoms at centre were coordinated by nitrogen atoms in aromatic rings and halogen ions. Differently, the structure of 6 was crystallized in orthorhombic space group (Pbcn, No. 60), formed 0-D dot structure (Fig.1,BC. Its lead atoms were linked to two 1,10-phen molecules and two iodine ions. The crystal structures could also be confirmed by other works [31–33]. Moreover, the measured powder XRD patterns of 1–7 were in good agreement with the simulated patterns from their single crystal structure. So, powder materials could represent them in our further studies. (see Fig. S2).

As a powerful tool for identifying the organic group, FTIR spectroscopy was further used for distinguishing 2,2’-bipy and 1,10-phen in different complexes. Fig. 2 A and B showed the FTIR spectra of complexes 1-4 with 2,2’-bipy and complexes 5-7 with 1,10-phen, respectively. All the complexes in Fig. 3 A exhibited four peaks around 1590, 1490, 1474, 1438
cm
- 1
assigned to the skeleton vibration of 2,2’-dipy ligand. On the other hand, complexes 5–7 exhibited another four peaks in the region of 600–900
cm
- 1
, which were assigned to the aromatic C-H asymmetric deformations of 1,10-phen (Fig. 2B). Furthermore, the SCN group also displayed obvious peak at 2055
cm
- 1
in complexes 4 and 7.

The study of the solid-state UV–vis-NIR diffuse reflectance spectra reflected the band gap of photosensitive materials and allowed us to optimize the excitation condition for their luminescent spectra. As shown in Fig. 3A, the band gaps of complexes 1–3 were estimated to 2.44 eV, 2.77 eV and 3.06 eV, respectively, which was coincide with the order in the periodic table (VIIA). As one kind of the pseudohalides, thiocyanate was selected as the inorganic ligand in complex 4 in order to explore the effect from the linear inorganic ligand. The bind gap of complex 4 in Fig. 3A was estimated to 2.96 eV near to complex 3, which implied the similar optical properties between the complexes with
SCN
-
and
Cl
-
inorganic ligands. When the organic ligand was changed into 1,10-phen, the complexes 5–7 with
I
-
and
SCN
-
were designed and synthesized. As shown in Fig. 3B, the band gaps of these complexes were estimated to 2.51 eV (5), 2.36 eV (6) and 2.91 eV (7), which were similar to their analogues with 2,2’-dipy ligand. Furthermore, all these complexes could be seen as the wide band-gap semiconductors, whose band gaps located in the region of 2–3 eV [34,35].

The luminescent properties were firstly discussed under excitation of 310 nm with a 450 W Xe lamp. Fig. 4 A showed solid emissions of complexes 1–4 with 2,2’-dipy ligand and different halogen ligand. We found 1 with
I
-
outputted the most bright emission around 595 nm and 2 with
Br
-
outputted the weak emission at the same wavelength. On the other hand, complexes 3 and 4 scarcely emitted any signals. All the luminescent properties also obeyed the periodic trends. Compared with the intensities of these complexes, complexes 1, 2 and 4 showed 13.4-fold, 3.5-fold and 1.1-fold enhancement at 595 nm than 3, respectively (Fig. 4B). With more rigid framework, the 1,10-phen ligand has enhanced the emission of the above complexes. As Fig. 4C shown, complex 5 with one 1,10-phen ligand and two
I
-
ligands exhibited stronger emission than 1. Its maximum emission peak hypsochromic shifted to 565 nm, which could be attributed to the decrease of intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) process in 1,10-phen. On the other hand, the complex 6 with additional 1,10-phen ligand only outputted weak emission at the longer wavelength. In order to confirm the periodic trends also affected in these complexes with 1,10-phen ligand, we also tested the emission of complex 7 with 1,10-phen and
SCN
-
ligands. A faint emission in Fig. 4C indicated our hypothesis was right. We used 7 as the control and the relative emission enhancement of 1 (4.8-fold), 5 (9.7-fold) and 6 (1.3-fold) were calculated in Fig. 4D, respectively. Furthermore, the fluorescent quantum yields of all complexes 1–7 were obtained as 0.096, 0.023, 0.003, 0.003, 0.125, 0.034 and 0.004, respectively. We also investigated the crystal emission through fluorescent microscopy. As shown in Fig. 4E, the complexes outputted the crystal emission with different colors and intensities. We could easily distinguish the emission from the yellow (6) to yellow-green (5) with tiny difference caused by the fixed filter of microscopy. As a consequence, our strategy successfully optimized the emission of Lead-complex combining with the rules of periodic trends and design of organic luminophores.

Depending on the TGA results, all these Lead-complexes showed outstanding thermal stability that provided the chance to develop a luminescent sensor for recording high temperature. As shown in Fig. 5 A, TGA data indicated that 1 and 2 showed higher decrease temperature (220 °C) than 3 and 4 (180 °C). Among them, complexes 1–3 experienced two weight loss steps from the start of the thermal decomposition to the complete end of the decomposition. According to the percentage of the initial residual weight (1: Calcd: 74.7%, Found:74.4%; 2: Calcd: 70.2%, Found: 70.4%; 3: Calcd: 64.1%, Found: 62.2%), it can be speculated that they decomposed to
PbI
2
,
PbBr
2
and
PbCl
2
, respectively.

In order to further validate this hypothesis, three complexes were heated at the temperature over their first decrease temperature for 3 h (1 at 300 °C, 2 at 305 °C, 3 at 250 °C). Then, the products were analysized by powder XRD compared with the standard
PbI
2
,
PbBr
2
and
PbCl
2
samples. As Fig. S3A–C shown, the XRD results of all three products were similar with their standard samples. Different from 1–3, 4 with
SCN
-
ligand showed multiple decomposition process initiating at 180 °C. Four steps were found in the whole decomposition of 4 at about 180, 215, 300 and 660 °C,which might imply several unstable intermediates assigned to the more complex
SCN
-
ligand. With the inconspicuous platform in its decomposition process, we calculated the weight loss of the principal residue at 660 °C. The result suggested that the principal residue was PbS for its 52.2% weight loss (Cal. for PbS: 50.1%). For validating its decrease product, 4 was also heated to 305 °C for 3 h, then, analyzed through the powder XRD. Compared to the simulated patterns of
PbS
, we could determine that its product was
PbS
(Fig. S3D). >As shown in Fig. 5B, TG analysis data indicate that 5 and 6 gave the early degradation at 330 °C and 230 °C, respectively. Furthermore, 6 could transfer into 5 over 230 °C. In this means, a wide temperature recording range could be constructed based on the 6–5 transformation process.

We also tested the TG curve of 7 with the same
SCN
-
ligand. As Fig. S4 shown, replacement of the 1,10-phen ligand, 7, significantly improved the thermal resistance up to the 270 °C. It suggested that the rigid organic ligand made the complex thermally more heat resistant than flexible 2,2’-dipy in the similar crystal structure.

Considering that 6 might transfer into 5 over 230 °C with irreversible process, a high temperature recorder was designed for recording the event of high temperature over 230 °C that ever happened in history (inset scheme of Fig. 6 ). Firstly, we optimized the excitation wavelength from 310 nm to 450 nm to obtain suitable emission of both complexes. Under excitation of 450 nm, 6 showed stronger orange emission around 592 nm than the yellow emission of 5 around 546 nm, which could be assigned to the effect of the more 1,10-phen ligand. According to the inset photos, the emissive color change could be distinguished by simple naked-eye pathway.

Furthermore, we heated 6 under different temperature around 200 °C to 270 °C. As shown in Fig. 7 , during 3 h, 6 had little change in intensity and no color change under 200 °C. When the temperature rise to 220
° C
, the emissive band shifted to 561 nm, which might be the merge of both 6 (592 nm) and 5 (546 nm). When the temperature raise to 230–270 °C, the emission of decomposed products shifted to 546 nm, which overlay the emission of 5. The temperature-dependent emissions had good overlap to the TGA results that evidenced our hypothesis.

All the complexes in this work could be easily crystallized. Further exploration of the temperature-induced emission changes was performed in the decomposition process of the bulk crystals of 6. Herein, 6 was heated under 270 °C that composited very fast in its powder state discussed before (Fig. 8C). However, 6 in crystal state shown more complex decomposition process with both color change and U shape intensity change. As shown in Fig. 10 , the emission intensity decreased very fast in former 10 min with the emission band moved from 592 nm to 560 nm. In above studies, we did not find such obvious emission quenching. So we believed this process indicated the effects from the collapse of the crystal structure, which always produced the bulk inclusions and quenched the luminescence from core. Further prolonging the time, the complex showed the recovery of emission intensity with good overlap of the emission band compared to the former study, which indicated that the complex completely collapsed into 5 under amorphous state. Furthermore, we drew the CIE1931 diagram taken into account this process. As shown in Fig. 10B, 6 gave the obvious colorimetric varied from orange (CIE: 0.394, 0.554) to yellow (CIE: 0.515, 0.472) that evidenced the naked-eye recognition strategy in our design. All the exploration discussed above was further evidenced by XRD patterns (Fig. S5). As a result, we have successfully obtained a useful sensor for luminescent responding a high temperature pulse.

In this work, a series of Lead-based luminescent complexes were synthesized for optimizing their luminescent properties and further being applied to record the high temperature history over 230 °C. All seven complexes were characterized by single crystal X-ray diffraction, powder XRD and FTIR technologies. With suitable optimizing strategy in both molecular engineering and excitation source, complexes 5 and 6 were developed as the recording pair, which showed strong emission and enough color difference discrimination with CIE coordinate at (0.515, 0.472) for 5 (yellow) and (0.394, 0.554) for 6 (orange). The TGA results evidenced that the additional organic ligand reduced the thermal stability of 6 compared to its analogue 5. The temperature-dependent luminescent responding models of 6 was further performed in harsh conditions with the temperature from 200 °C to 270 °C in different time scale. The results indicated 6 could record the high temperature history in the range of 230 °C–270 °C through an irreversible transition to 5 with the partial degradation of organic ligand. We believe these results provided a valuable sensing approach for high temperature in industrial processes and a suitable designing strategy for luminescent Lead-complexes in several practical fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by NSFC (21404068, 51502169).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, athttps://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2018.10.028.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

Hg

ions with enhanced detection sensitivity

/

-codoped

4

,

VO

group to
